data.title: DHS says there's body cam footage of Pretti shooting
data.description:
The Department of Homeland Security (DHS) said multiple Customs and Border Protection officers were wearing body cameras during the fatal shooting of protester Alex Pretti on Saturday in Minneapolis. 

Camera footage from various angles is being reviewed by investigators, a DHS official told The Hill’s partner network NewsNation.

Outrage from lawmakers, former presidents and the public have bubbled after bystander footage showed Pretti being shot after recording with his phone. 

Greg Bongino, commander-at-large of the Border Patrol, accused Pretti of approaching officers with a handgun.

“The victims are the Border Patrol agents. I’m not blaming the Border Patrol agents,” Bovino said during a Sunday interview with CNN’s Dana Bash. 

“The victims are the Border Patrol agents. The suspect put himself in that situation,” he added.

President Trump in response to outrage sent border czar Tom Homan to Minnesota to provide a “fair” response to increased demonstrations.

“I am sending Tom Homan to Minnesota tonight. He has not been involved in that area, but knows and likes many of the people there,” Trump wrote Monday on Truth Social, adding that he had a good call with Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz (D) earlier in the day. 

“Tom is tough but fair and will report to me,” Trump wrote.

data.title: Democrats can and should use a shutdown threat to abolish ICE
data.description:
As the Jan. 30 funding deadline approaches, Washington is bracing for a familiar ritual: the government shutdown standoff. But this time, the stakes are different, and the political math has fundamentally shifted. For Democratic leadership, the choice is no longer just about keeping the lights on. It’s recognizing a rare moment of alignment between moral necessity and hard-nosed pragmatism.

Democrats should not fear a shutdown. In fact, they should refuse to reopen the government until Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) is abolished. While such a stance was once labeled radical, the reality of 2026 suggests it is now the most practical exit strategy for both parties.

For years, the abolish ICE movement was treated as part of the political fringe. Today, it is a reflection of the median voter’s fatigue. Recent polling from The New York Times and Siena University paints a devastating picture for the agency: 61 percent of American voters now disapprove of ICE’s tactics, including 71 percent of Independents.  

The killings of Renee Good and Alex Pretti in Minneapolis this month, both captured on video and seen by most of the public, have served as a breaking point. When a majority of Americans, including a significant portion of Independents, view a federal agency’s tactics as unjustified and too forceful, that agency can no longer be an effective tool of public safety. 

While Republicans have historically defended ICE, the agency is becoming brand poison for the GOP. Under the weight of Operation Metro Surge, the visuals of militarized federal agents in American suburbs have alienated the very swing voters the Republican Party needs to win elections. 

For Republicans, ICE is now a political albatross. Every day a federal agent is filmed in a high-profile confrontation in a city like Minneapolis or Portland, Maine, the Republican “law and order” message is undermined by the reality of federal overreach. By forcing a shutdown over ICE funding, Democrats would be offering Republicans an off-ramp from a policy of blind support that is dragging down their approval ratings.

The practical argument for abolition isn’t about ending border security, it’s about a political reset on the issue. Abolishing ICE allows for the reallocation of its budget toward more effective, accountable and less controversial agencies.

Rep. Seth Moulton’s (D-Mass.) recent proposal to divert these funds toward lapsed Affordable Care Act tax credits is a prime example of a practical shift. It trades a high-conflict, low-approval agency for popular, tangible benefits that stabilize the economy. Folding essential ICE duties into the Department of Justice or traditional Customs and Border Protection enforcement roles would remove the rogue culture that has come to define the agency, replacing it with standard professional oversight.

Critics may point to the long odds of passing such a bill, given the number of votes needed and the likelihood of President Trump vetoing it. But doing so would put the president in an impossible spot. If he vetoes a bill that lowers health care premiums for millions just to protect an agency that only hardcore MAGA supports, he hands the initiative right back to his opponents. 

A veto isn’t a dead end, it’s a trap. It would force more Republican senators in moderate states to go on the record. They would have to explain to their constituents why they’re keeping the government shuttered and health care expensive just to defend a federal agency accused of being responsible for deaths of two Americans and is refusing local investigative cooperation. 

Forcing that veto-proof override vote is the point. I suspect many Republicans are privately seeking an exit strategy from the administration’s immigration enforcement tactics, but they need the cover of a governing crisis to finally distance themselves from the White House. By holding the line, Democrats provide that permission structure. They make it a choice between violence and chaos and practical, funded governance.

Democrats have the leverage of the Jan. 30 deadline. They should use it to demand a total freeze on ICE funding as a bridge to its eventual dissolution. If the government stays closed, the blame will fall on those fighting to protect an agency operating at an unrecoverable trust deficit, pushing out transparent propaganda that Republican politicians are struggling to coherently defend. 

In the high-stakes poker game of D.C. appropriations, you only bet big when you have the cards. With public opinion trending sharply towards a fundamental overhaul of immigration enforcement, Democrats aren’t just holding a winning hand, they’re holding the only hand that leads to a stable, long-term resolution — reassuring a weary public that the rule of law stands and that the separation of powers is once again intact. 

Logan McMillen works in library information services and studies the history of authoritarianism. His writing has previously appeared in The New Republic and The Minnesota Star Tribune.

data.title: Aircraft carrier USS Abraham Lincoln in Middle East, expanding Trump military options against Iran
data.description:
The aircraft carrier USS Abraham Lincoln and its three accompanying warships have sailed into the Middle East region, providing President Trump additional military firepower if he decides to strike Iran. 

The USS Abraham Lincoln, which is equipped with Mk 57 Mod3 Sea Sparrow surface-to-air missile launchers and can carry squadrons of bombers and early warning planes, and its strike group are in the U.S. Central Command (Centcom) area, which encompasses the Middle East region, the command confirmed Monday.

The addition of the carrier in the region comes as Trump warned Thursday that the U.S. is sending an “armada” toward Iran following nationwide protests in the country, where thousands of demonstrators have been killed by authorities. 

“We have a big flotilla going in that direction,” the president told reporters aboard Air Force One. “We have a big force going toward Iran. I’d rather not see anything happen, but we’re watching them very closely.”

Over the weekend, the commander of Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps, Gen. Mohammad Pakpour, said the nation has its “finger on the trigger” at the U.S. following Trump’s warning. 

“The Islamic Revolutionary Guard and dear Iran stand more ready than ever, finger on the trigger, to execute the orders and directives of the Commander-in-Chief,” Pakpour said on Saturday. 

Trump refrained from authorizing strikes on Iran as the demonstrations against the regime intensified earlier this month, and he said last week that he stopped Iran’s leadership from hanging 837 people.

The death toll is currently unclear as large swaths of the country are affected by the internet blackout, but a human rights group, HRANA, has reported over 5,800 confirmed deaths since the protests broke out in late December. 

The Lincoln, a Nimitz-Class nuclear-powered carrier, can house more than 5,000 personnel and carry EA-18G Growler electronic-warfare jets, F/A-18 jet planes and F-35Cs. Its Navy destroyers can fire long-range Tomahawk cruise missiles.

data.title: Major airline officially ends longstanding seat policy: 'Everything majorly changes'
data.description:
(NEXSTAR) – Travelers flying with Southwest Airlines are preparing to adjust to the airline’s new assigned-seating policy, which replaces the carrier’s longstanding open-seating arrangement as of Jan. 27.

“Southwest Airlines has no assigned seats for one more day,” one traveler flying to Kansas City remarked in an X post on Monday. “On my way back, everything majorly changes as I already have my assigned seat.”

What’s changing?

Southwest had first announced plans to ditch its longstanding open-seating policy in July 2024, in part to boost revenue. But the company also claimed its passengers increasingly preferred an assigned seating model with premium seating options.

“Our Customers want more choice and greater control over their travel experience,” Southwest executive Tony Roach said in 2025. “Assigned seating unlocks new opportunities for our Customers — including the ability to select Extra Legroom seats — and removes the uncertainty of not knowing where they will sit in the cabin.”

In the summer of 2025, Southwest also began booking assigned seats for passengers whose itineraries included flights after the Jan. 27 start date.

Some passengers appeared to welcome the change, saying it would cut down on “bad passenger behavior” and “abuses of open seating.” Others said it gave them a sense of certainty about the boarding process.

“I for one am SOOOOOOOOO happy to be rid of that open seating nonsense,” one X user wrote. “At 6’5″, I will GLADLY pay a premium for my favorite exit row seat with the leg room and not need to stand in line like cattle.”

Others, however, were less enthused. Critics argued that open-seating was preferable for guests who booked in groups, or passengers who had to change flights last-minute (as they wouldn’t be immediately relegated to the least-preferable seats on the plane, as might have otherwise happened on a different airline). Some felt the switch to assigned seating was just a money-making tactic.

“They had a good run but it’s obvious corporate greed won over what we all felt made them the best airline,” one social-media user claimed.

A spokesperson for Southwest was not immediately available to comment. But on social media, a representative for the carrier responded to a traveler who appeared upset by the changes.

“We’re evolving to meet changing Customer preferences, & our research clearly indicates a desire for assigned seating,” the representative wrote in an X response. “Still, we know assigned seating is not preferable for every Customer. We hope you’ll give us an opportunity to welcome you onboard.”

How does Southwest’s assigned seating work?

Customers booking flights with Southwest are now instructed to select from three seating options: “Standard” seats, located near the back of the cabin; “Preferred” seats, located nearer the front; and “Extra Legroom” seats, which are located near the exit rows and offer between 3 and 5 inches of extra legroom.

The type of seat a passenger purchases determines when they board, or whether they’re eligible for other perks, Southwest’s website explains. The changes also mean slight tweaks to Southwest’s pre-boarding policies for active-duty military members or families with children.

What other policies are changing?

In addition to its seating policy, Southwest on Tuesday is also changing its policy for “customers of size” who wish to use an extra seat.

Before Jan. 27, plus-size passengers were not required to purchase a second seat in advance, though it was recommended. (Additional seats for larger customers were provided free of charge “if space is available on the flight,” according to Southwest.) Refunds could be obtained after travel.

But beginning on Jan. 27, customers must buy an extra seat and “pay any applicable seat fee” before boarding. Refunds can still be obtained after travel, but eligibility will depend on a number of specific conditions, namely, that both seats were in the same fare class, the refund request was made within 90 days of travel, and the plane would have taken off with “at least one” extra open seat, according to Southwest.

Plus-size passengers can also be refunded if the plane was full, but the manifest included passengers who boarded with “space available passes” — e.g., passes given to airline employees or other non-paying passengers who are traveling for leisure.

After announcing the new policy earlier this year, advocates for plus-size travelers were disappointed by the policy change, with one group — the National Association to Advance Fat Acceptance — telling The New York Times that Southwest had been a “beacon of hope for many fat people who otherwise wouldn’t have been flying.”

Despite the upcoming changes to Southwest’s plus-size seating policy, the carrier is still the only major U.S. airline (out of American, Delta and United) that offers any type of refund for eligible plus-size passengers after the flight.

Earlier this year, Southwest said its agents were attempting to notify former fliers of the new policy in advance of their next trip with the airline.

“To ensure space, we are communicating to Customers who have previously used the extra seat policy they should purchase it at booking,” a spokesperson said.

data.title: January, not April, may be the cruelest month
data.description:
In 1922, with World War I tragically still fresh in too many minds, American-born British poet T.S. Eliot wrote in “The Waste Land” that “April is the cruelest month.” But today in America, January may prove to be the cruelest of all. 

Why? January is the convergence of three highly disruptive and destructive forces. The first is the budget deadline. The continuing resolution expires at month’s end. It is not a good sign that the Senate was out of session last week. Suppose Congress cannot agree and is deadlocked. The government will shut down again, possibly for longer than the 43 days last fall.

Although it is in no one’s interest for this to happen again, Democrats and Republicans may be unable to compromise on what programs should be cut or funded. Health care is obviously a 5,000-pound gorilla. Huge increases for medical insurance have taken hold, by itself making this an explosive issue with the political effect of a thermonuclear bomb.

What people do not understand is the pernicious damage a continuing resolution does. The uncertainty of when a full budget is finally approved and signed into law makes planning virtually impossible for the executive branch.

Every day a continuing resolution is in place, the government loses about 15 percent of its purchasing power. In the corporate world, any company that operated this way would be out of business. But this is the way our government operates, as incredible as it may sound. 

And at the end of the month, disruptive force number two activates — or, in this case, does not. On Feb. 5, the New START nuclear arms control agreement permanently expires. New START kept U.S. and Russian strategic nuclear weapons limited to 1,550 warheads each. The treaty cannot be extended, although Moscow and Washington could honor those limits.

That is not the same guarantee as a legal document. Russia, which has been noncompliant with New START since 2022, could more easily expand its warhead count than the U.S. And the Trump administration’s emphasis on Golden Dome defense that could counter most or all attacks against the U.S. will almost certainly cause Moscow to increase its number of warheads and nuclear delivery systems.

Do we really want another nuclear arms race, especially when our replacement submarine, missile and bomber force replacements will cost many hundreds if not trillions of dollars? The answer would seem obvious.

Then there was Greenland, the third force. President Donald Trump has long held that Greenland is vital to American defenses. His arguments are not self-evident. The idea that the Arctic is strategically more vital due to the melting of ice and new sea routes that will cut transport costs substantially is fine. But the U.S. already has Alaska on the Arctic, as well as nearly unlimited restrictions on increasing its forces on Greenland, based on a 1951 treaty. And Russia and China are not that heavily engaged yet.

After Trump threatened to use military force to seize Greenland, the stock markets reacted and recorded huge losses. At the World Economic Forum last week, Trump withdrew the threat of military intervention. But the damage had already been done, even though the markets recovered. NATO’s Article Five states that an attack against one member is considered an attack against all. Greenland, belonging to Denmark, is part of NATO. Had anyone in the White House informed the president of the implications of his threat? Probably not.

With that matter now resolved, the reactions of European members of NATO cannot be reversed. This is not 1956, when NATO allies Britain and France, in concert with Israel, occupied the Suez Canal and defeated the Egyptian army. President Dwight D. Eisenhower rebuked America’s NATO allies in that case and the invading forces were withdrawn. The alliance was shaken as a consequence.

Now, the threat to invade Greenland has similarly shaken the alliance. The reason is simple: Allies can no longer depend on the U.S. under Trump. And this may be unfixable, no matter who is elected president in 2028. Trump, having additionally invited Russian President Vladimir Putin to join the Gaza peace panel, may accomplish what five Soviet and three Russian leaders failed to do: end the NATO alliance.

Arms control, budgets and Greenland have been unlikely cohorts and comrades in challenging America. How this turns out obviously is a work in progress. But I do not believe this will turn out well.

Harlan Ullman, Ph.D., is UPI’s Arnaud deBorchgrave Distinguished Columnist, a senior advisor at the Atlantic Council, the chairman of two private companies and the principal author of the doctrine of shock and awe. He and former United Kingdom Defense Chief David Richards are the authors of a forthcoming book on preventing strategic catastrophe.

data.title: 7 killed in Maine jet crash during snowstorm
data.description:
The Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) on Monday said seven people were killed in a crash of a private jet in Maine, leaving one survivor.

Officials said the Bombardier Challenger 600 crashed at 7:45 p.m. Sunday, while 10 inches of snow were on the ground, according to The Associated Press.

The FAA and the National Transportation Safety Board are investigating the cause of the incident.

The jet was registered to a corporation that shares the same address in Houston as the personal injury law firm Arnold & Itkin, AP reported. Officials would not comment on the plane’s destination.

“Aircraft upside down. We have a passenger aircraft upside down,” someone said in an audio recording of air traffic controllers, 45 seconds after the jet took off. 

A team of first responders visited the site of the crash within a minute, Bangor International Airport Director Jose Saavedra told reporters during a press conference.

“This is normal for us to deal with weather events, and we had crews on site to address the weather event we had ongoing,”  Saavedra said, noting other commercial and private planes were landing and departing during that incident. 

The incident took place while the aircraft was departing from Bangor International Airport in Penobscot County, Maine, and it experienced a fire after the crash, according to AP.

The airport will be closed until around noon Wednesday.

data.title: Live updates: Immigration crackdown lands in Minnesota court as Trump speaks to Walz
data.description:
Another deadly shooting by federal immigration enforcement officials in Minneapolis has shaken the country, and it may push Washington to shut down part of the government later this week.

The killing of 37-year-old Alex Pretti by a Customs and Border Patrol agent on Saturday has renewed uproar nationally, and Senate Democrats have said they will vote against the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) funding package as Jan. 30 shutdown deadline looms.

Some Republican lawmakers are joining Democrats in calling for an impartial investigation into Pretti’s shooting.

A federal judge in Minneapolis is hearing arguments Monday as the state of Minnesota sues the Trump administration, saying its immigration raid tactics are unconstitutional.

This morning, President Trump said he spoke with Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz (D-Minn.) and that he would send border czar Tom Homan to the state to begin managing Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) actions there.

“It was a very good call, and we, actually, seemed to be on a similar wavelength,” Trump wrote on social media, noting that Walz was “happy” that Homan would be in Minnesota.

On Capitol Hill this week, the House is out, but the Senate is in, and it must pass spending bills to avert a government shutdown.

Trump on Monday will hold policy meetings and do a radio interview. Press secretary Karoline Leavitt will brief reporters at 1 p.m.

Follow live below.

data.title: Gun groups, GOP question Minneapolis shooting
data.description:
12:30 Report is The Hill’s midday newsletter. Subscribe here or using the box below:

Close

Thank you for signing up!

Subscribe to more newsletters here

The latest in politics and policy. Direct to your inbox. Sign up for the 12:30 Report newsletter

It’s Monday. Washington is essentially shut down today because of Sunday’s snow and ice storm. Did you get hit by the storm? If so, how much snow did you get? Let me know!

In today’s issue:

  • Second fatal ICE shooting ignites national uproar
  • Gun groups question Trump officials’ rationale
  • President sends border czar to Minnesota
  • Chances of partial government shutdown skyrocket

🪧 TALK OF THE MORNING

An uproar in Minnesota:

In the past three weeks, federal officers have fatally shot two U.S. citizens in Minnesota. The latest incident happened over the weekend when Alex Pretti, a 37-year-old intensive-care nurse at a Veterans Affairs hospital, was shot and killed by a Border Patrol agent.

The Trump administration rushed to describe Pretti as a “domestic terrorist,” stressing that he had a gun. But bystander cell-phone videos, which have been analyzed by major news organizations frame-by-frame, appear to tell a different story.

Pretti in the footage is seen holding his cellphone, not a gun. The video appears to show an officer taking Pretti’s gun and walking away before Pretty was shot. Ten shots were fired, according to audio analysis.

The New York Times’s video analysis

The Wall Street Journal’s video analysis

The Washington Post’s video analysis

The Minneapolis police chief said that Pretti is believed to have been a legal gun owner with a permit to carry. He also said he hasn’t seen any evidence that Pretti brandished his gun.

The competing comments from Trump administration officials versus local officials have ignited a national uproar.

Republicans have started raising concerns about the fatal shooting. A growing number of Republican lawmakers is calling for impartial investigation into the shooting.

Leading gun rights groups in the U.S. are even questioning the Trump administration’s stance, noting that people have a right to carry a weapon with a permit.

A top prosecutor in the U.S. Attorney’s Office in Los Angeles argued that “if you approach law enforcement with a gun, there is a high likelihood they will be legally justified in shooting you. Don’t do it!”

The National Rifle Association called those comments “dangerous and wrong.” And Gun Owners of America also condemned the comments.

President Trump says he is sending White House border czar Tom Homan to Minnesota later today in the wake of the shooting. And he says he spoke with Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz (D-Minn.) this morning. He described the call as “very good.”

What else has the president said about the shooting?: He briefly spoke with The Wall Street Journal and declined to say whether the federal officer acted appropriately, saying his administration is reviewing the incident.

💬 Follow today’s live blog

➤ NOTABLE REACTIONS FROM REPUBLICANS:

Rep. James Comer (R-Ky.): The House Oversight and Government Reform Committee chair says Trump should consider pulling ICE personnel from Minnesota.

Sen. Bill Cassidy (R-La.): “The events in Minneapolis are incredibly disturbing. The credibility of ICE and DHS are at stake. There must be a full joint federal and state investigation. We can trust the American people with the truth,” he wrote in a social media post.

Sen. Lisa Murkowski (R-Alaska): Murkowski says the shooting raises “serious questions within the administration about the adequacy of immigration-enforcement training and the instructions officers are given on carrying out their mission.” She also says federal immigration officers “do not have carte blanche” while carrying out their operations.

Vermont’s Republican governor, Phil Scott, railed against the shooting: “Enough … It’s not acceptable for American citizens to be killed by federal agents for exercising their God-given and constitutional rights to protest their government,” Scott said in a statement. “At best, these federal immigration operations are a complete failure of coordination of acceptable public safety and law enforcement practices, training, and leadership.” Read more

MTG: Former Rep. Marjorie Taylor Greene (R-Ga.) urged Trump’s MAGA base to “take off their blinders,” arguing Americans “are all being incited into civil war.”

➤ NOTABLE REACTIONS FROM DEMOCRATS:

From Senate Democratic Leader Chuck Schumer (N.Y.): “What’s happening in Minnesota is appalling — and unacceptable in any American city,” he said in a statement.

Some Democrats are calling for Noem’s impeachment: Including Sen. Jacky Rosen (D-Nev.). “Kristi Noem and her department’s latest attempt to mislead the American public regarding the brutal and unjustified killing of Alex Pretti is deeply shameful, and she must be impeached and removed from office immediately,” Rosen said in a statement.

Several Democrats regret voting to fund DHS: Including Rep. Tom Suozzi (N.Y.), who was one of the seven House Democrats to vote with Republicans to fund the government. “I failed to view the DHS funding vote as a referendum on the illegal and immoral conduct of ICE in Minneapolis,” Suozzi wrote in an email. “I have long been critical of ICE’s unlawful behavior and I must do a better job of demonstrating that.”

➤ HAPPENING TODAY:

A federal judge in Minneapolis is hearing arguments today on the legality of the Trump administration’s immigration tactics. The state of Minnesota is suing, arguing the tactics are unconstitutional.

➤ RELATED READS:

The Atlantic: Welcome to the American Winter: In the frozen streets of Minneapolis, something profound is happening.

Op-ed in The New York Times: ‘We Are Creating the Conditions for a Catastrophe.’ Three Columnists on Minneapolis.

The Hill: Republicans divided on ICE tactics as shutdown looms

Op-ed in The Wall Street Journal: Time for ICE to Pause in Minneapolis

⏱️ ON CAPITOL HILL 

Part of the government is now very likely to shut down:

The killing of Alex Pretti has outraged Senate Democrats, who now refuse to fund the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) because it includes funding for Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE).

Lawmakers had been on the verge of passing the remaining government funding bills, which expire on Jan. 30, but this incident throws that into question. That means the chances of a government shutdown at the end of the week skyrocket.

Read Al Weaver’s reporting on where things stand

Today’s Morning Report went deeper on the odds of a shutdown. Read it here and sign up to get the newsletter in your inbox each weekday morning: Click here to sign up

🌨️ OTHER NEWS

The storm that keeps on giving:

A thick coat of ice and snow has coated much of the Southeastern United States.

More than 15,000 flights have been delayed today and more than 4,400 have been canceled so far after a massive winter storm pummeled the U.S.

More than 800,000 Americans across the Southeast have lost power as of this morning.

🗺️ The latest Weather Channel forecast

➤ QUICK HITS:

— President Trump says he won’t attend the Super Bowl this year.

— The body of the last hostage in Gaza has been recovered, clearing the way for the next phase of the peace deal.

➤ MORE READS:

The Washington Post: I let ChatGPT analyze a decade of my Apple Watch data. Then I called my doctor.

The New Yorker: The Battle for Minneapolis

New York Magazine: The Superhuman President

COMING UP

The House and Senate are out. President Trump is in Washington. (All times EST)

1 p.m. Trump meets with the secretary of the Air Force.

1 p.m. White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt briefs reporters. 💻 Livestream

2:30 p.m. Trump participates in policy meetings in the Oval Office.

4:30 p.m. Trump does a radio interview.

INTERNET BUZZ

☕ Celebrate: Today is National Irish Coffee Day.

🖌️ Want to see all the changes Trump made to the White House?: The New York Times published a fascinating graphic showing before-and-after photos of all the changes Trump has made to the White House.

🧗 The talk of social media: An American rock climber scaled one of the tallest skyscrapers in the world without any safety equipment. It was streamed live on Netflix.

AND FINALLY…

And because this made me smile on a tense day, check out this dog sending messages in his sleep.

data.title: Opposing Trump's quest for power is not about politics, but a defense of democracy
data.description:
The deeply troubling criminal investigation of Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell regarding costly renovations of the Fed’s headquarters has caused a widespread backlash that transcends political differences because it threatens the central bank’s independence. Unfortunately, President Trump’s attack on the Fed is part of a much larger power grab that has not provoked a similar response.

Our democracy and future depend not only on opposing Trump’s reckless actions, but on the public understanding that opposition to Trump is not a partisan fight but rather one about the future of our democracy.

When I founded my nonpartisan organization nearly 25 years ago, it was not a challenge to find partners and leaders of all political stripes to agree with our mission to help build a better government by supporting federal employees so that agencies can more effectively meet the needs of all Americans.

We never doubted that while the political leaders whose administrations we worked closely with — both Republican and Democrat — might fundamentally disagree about the size and role of the federal government, they would always agree that whatever the government does, it should do it well and on behalf of the people.

During the last year, Trump has shattered this fundamental consensus. He rejects the premise that our government exists to serve the public interest. He rejects the 140-year-old notion that federal employees should be hired based on merit and experience, not on political considerations. He rejects the very idea that public service is a worthwhile and necessary endeavor.

In place of those long-held beliefs, Trump has built a presidency, in his image alone, of unrestrained power, enrichment for himself and his supporters and endless punishment for his perceived enemies. This is the path to an imperial presidency.

In the face of all this, my organization and I have found ourselves often doing something we have, with few exceptions, steadfastly resisted in the 25 years we’ve been doing this work: speaking out, vocally, against the actions of a president and his administration just as a wide range of lawmakers and finance leaders recently have done in the case of the Federal Reserve.

Our opposition is not politically motivated, but an attempt to sound the alarm about the damage being done to our nation’s central democratic institutions, including the effort to politicize the civil service and haphazardly deplete it of unbiased expertise and talent.

We have opposed Trump’s efforts to usurp the role of Congress by refusing to spend appropriated funds and we have spoken out against the arbitrary shuttering and sabotage of agencies as well as the use of law enforcement to punish his adversaries. We are not taking a political stand, but objecting because a healthy democracy requires a government capable of doing its job while respecting the rule of law and the separation of powers.

Trump has succeeded in his destruction in part because he uses the language rather than the fundamentals of good government to cloak his power grab in something not offensive. He says he is fighting waste and fraud when he is causing them. He says he is stopping the weaponization of our government when he is pursuing exactly that end.

Take the firing of federal employees and the effort to replace career experts in government with political loyalists as an example. Prior to Trump’s return to office in 2025, a poll by my organization showed that 87 percent of Americans — Democrats, Republicans and independents — agreed that “a nonpartisan civil service is important for having a strong American democracy.” Today, 66 percent agree with that statement, a huge drop driven mostly by loss of support from Republicans and independents.

Trump managed to hold Republicans together during the government shutdown in October and November under a banner of partisanship while he pursued an effort to punish his perceived enemies, withholding federal funds from uncooperative cities, laying off civil servants and threatening not to pay furloughed federal workers. On the surface this looks like partisan gamesmanship, but it is not about party at all. It is yet another example of Trump punishing opponents, including the federal workforce he sees as an impediment to his vision of an all-powerful presidency.

The right response to Trump’s attempts to use the cloak of partisanship to amass unfettered personal control and weaken the government in his own self-interest is to reject his political framing. Federal employees who resigned rather than take acts they saw as unlawful or immoral did not do it because they are Democrats. They did it because they are democrats.

The fight to stop our slide toward autocracy begins with all of us embracing that distinction.

Max Stier is the founding president and CEO of the nonpartisan, nonprofit Partnership for Public Service, an organization focused on building a better government and a stronger democracy.

data.title: Bovino tangles with Republicans, Democrats on X after latest Minnesota shooting
data.description:
Gregory Bovino, commander-at-large of the U.S. Border Patrol, tangled with Democrats and Republicans alike online in the wake of another fatal shooting in Minneapolis by federal immigration authorities.

In a Saturday post on the social platform X, Sen. Bill Cassidy (R-La.) expressed concern about Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) and the Department of Homeland Security’s (DHS) reputation and called for “a full joint federal and state investigation” into the recent shooting.

“The same state that refuses to work with ICE is now going to ‘investigate’?  How about investigating a certain mayor who told cops to fight ICE in the streets. You don’t seem concerned about that – how come, senator?” Bovino said in a response to Cassidy’s post on X.

In response to an X post on Saturday from Rep. Eric Swalwell (D-Calif) encouraging ICE officers to “[w]alk off the job,” Bovino replied that he “was thinking the same for” the California representative.

Bovino’s posts came in the wake of Saturday’s fatal shooting of Alex Pretti, a 37-year-old intensive care nurse who lived in Minneapolis, by a Customs and Border Protection (CBP) agent. Pretti’s shooting was justified, according to authorities, with the 37-year-old allegedly coming up to them with a “9mm semi-automatic” handgun. Eyewitness videos from the scene of the shooting show Pretti holding a phone.

Pretti’s death came only a few weeks after the fatal shooting of Renee Good by an ICE officer in the same city. There has been criticism of President Trump’s immigration agenda throughout his second term, but both shootings have sparked mass outrage toward the administration over how it handles immigration.

Rep. Thomas Massie (R-Ky.), who has been on the outs with the Trump administration in the last year, said in a post on X on Sunday that “[c]arrying a firearm is not a death sentence, it’s a Constitutionally protected God-given right, and if you don’t understand this you have no business in law enforcement or government.”

“Attacking law enforcement is not a right like you want it to be,” Bovino responded to the Kentucky Republican.

Sen. Mark Kelly (D-Ariz.), in his own X post on Saturday, encouraged his followers to “[b]elieve what you see.”

“Alex Pretti was trying to help a woman off the ground. Then immigration agents tackled, shot, and killed him,” Kelly added. “It’s time for them to get the hell out of MN.”

“You were there? Typical arm chair quarterback,” Bovino responded to Kelly.

The Hill has reached out to Kelly’s, Massie’s, Swalwell’s and Cassidy’s offices for comment.
